text:
While the core objective of any 'omics science is the comprehensive measurement of all biological constituents, achieving such breadth in metabolomics is uniquely difficult. The metabolome consists of a chemically diverse array of small molecules with vastly different physical properties, ranging from highly polar sugar phosphates to hydrophobic lipids. Traditional profiling methods often introduce a distinct bias: reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC) excels at capturing lipophilic compounds but fails to retain water-soluble ones, while hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography (HILIC) reverses this limitation.

In this study, we developed an integrative workflow that bridges these gaps by combining RPLC/MS and HILIC/MS profiling from a single biological extract. We first optimized an aminopropyl-based HILIC method by evaluating various mobile-phase additives and pH levels to ensure high sensitivity and technical reproducibility. When applied to perturbed bacterial metabolomes, this HILIC approach accurately captured key dysregulated metabolites in central carbon pathways—such as amino acids, organic acids, and phosphorylated sugars—that were otherwise lost in the RPLC void volume.

To demonstrate the utility of this dual-separation approach, we analyzed bacterial cells, human plasma, and cancer cells. Using a single extraction protocol (80% organic solvent), we successfully detected over 30,000 features per sample type, spanning both lipid and central carbon metabolism. Our data reveals that RPLC provides the highest unique feature coverage in ESI positive mode, whereas HILIC contributes the most unique information in ESI negative mode. We conclude that when sample volume and time are constrained, the most informative "snapshot" of the metabolome is achieved by pairing RPLC (ESI+) with HILIC (ESI-).
